reasoning: The market requires at least 10,000 completely driverless, L4 ride-hailing trips (Waymo-style, no safety driver, no observers, no employee testers, >400m) by the end of 2025. Historically, Elon Musk has repeatedly promised L4/Robotaxi capabilities "next year" or within a few months (e.g., 2019, 2020) and has systematically failed to meet these timelines due to the immense technical challenges of L4 unmonitored driving. Achieving 10,000 non-tester L4 trips requires not just a technical breakthrough in Tesla's vision-only system to L4 readiness without safety drivers, but also setting up a public-facing ride-hailing network and assuming full L4 liability. While Texas regulations are permissive and Tesla could technically rush a limited geofenced setup, the strict requirements of "no testers" and "no observers" paired with 10k cumulative public rides by EOY make this highly unlikely. Given Musk's track record of ambitious but delayed autonomous driving targets, I assign a 10% probability.
